title: First MOTION to Dismiss by Defendant(s) Michelino P Sunseri. (Attachments: # 1 Supplement Brief)(Rienzie, Alexander) (Entered: 04/08/2025)

ecf_meta.title: First MOTION to Dismiss by Defendant(s) Michelino P Sunseri. (Attachments: # 1 Supplement Brief)(Rienzie, Alexander) (Entered: 04/08/2025)
ecf_meta.raw_docket_line: First MOTION to Dismiss by Defendant(s) Michelino P Sunseri. (Attachments: # 1 Supplement Brief)(Rienzie, Alexander) (Entered: 04/08/2025)

analysis.summary_one_line: Defendant moves to dismiss the violation notice with prejudice on failure-to-state-an-offense, equal protection, due process, and nondelegation grounds.
analysis.summary_paragraph: Sunseri moves to dismiss the Violation Notice (ECF 1) with prejudice under FRCrP 12(b)(1) and (3)(A), advancing four theories: (A) failure to state an offense; (B) Fifth Amendment equal protection (selective enforcement); (C) Fifth Amendment due process (vagueness/notice); and (D) violation of U.S. Const. Art. I § 1 for unconstitutional delegation of legislative power. The motion is supported by a separately filed brief (Attachment 1). It signals the defense's principal pretrial dispositive challenge, attacking both the regulatory framework under 36 CFR § 2.1(b) and the prosecution's charging decision.
analysis.holdings_or_relief: Defendant requests dismissal of the violation notice with prejudice on four grounds: failure to state an offense, equal protection, due process, and unconstitutional delegation of legislative power under Article I § 1.

key_quotes.text: Defendant Michelino Sunseri respectfully requests this Court dismiss the violation notice filed against him (ECF 1) with prejudice.
violation of U.S. const. Art. I. § 1 for unconstitutional delegation of legislative power

title: First MOTION to Dismiss by Defendant(s) Michelino P Sunseri. (Attachments: # 1 Supplement Brief)(Rienzie, Alexander) (Entered: 04/08/2025)

ecf_meta.title: First MOTION to Dismiss by Defendant(s) Michelino P Sunseri. (Attachments: # 1 Supplement Brief)(Rienzie, Alexander) (Entered: 04/08/2025)
ecf_meta.raw_docket_line: First MOTION to Dismiss by Defendant(s) Michelino P Sunseri. (Attachments: # 1 Supplement Brief)(Rienzie, Alexander) (Entered: 04/08/2025)

analysis.summary_one_line: Defendant moves to dismiss the violation notice on failure-to-state, selective-enforcement, inadequate-notice, and nondelegation grounds.
analysis.summary_paragraph: Defense brief in support of Rule 12(b)(1) and (3) motion to dismiss the 36 CFR 2.1(b) charge with prejudice. Four theories: (A) the government cannot prove a valid trail closure under 36 CFR §§ 1.5 and 1.7 because there is no written determination or Federal Register rulemaking for the 'old climber's trail,' and signage is inadequate compared to nearby Delta Lake; (B) selective enforcement violating Fifth Amendment equal protection, evidenced by 57 Strava users posting 83 runs on the trail, four uncited prior FKT speed records (including Kilian Jornet in 2012), and Ranger Bellino's alleged statement 'We want to drag you in front of a judge and make you apologize'; (C) inadequate notice violating due process; and (D) unconstitutional delegation of legislative power under Art. I § 1—the Organic Act of 1916 supplies no intelligible principle to support criminal penalties under 18 U.S.C. § 1865(a). References the parallel BASE Access v. NPS lawsuit. Notes plea offers included a five-year park ban that would force Sunseri to relocate from Victor, ID.
analysis.holdings_or_relief: Defendant requests dismissal with prejudice of the 36 CFR 2.1(b) violation notice (ECF 1) on four independent grounds: invalid closure, selective enforcement, inadequate notice, and unconstitutional delegation of legislative power.

key_quotes.text: "We want to drag you in front of a judge and make you apologize"—ignoring Defendant's prior efforts to "[rectify] this situation" and forcing involvement of counsel.
key_quotes.context: alleged Ranger Bellino statement, Sept. 25, 2024 call

key_quotes.text: 36 CFR § 2.1(b) is not intended to prevent "off-trail exploring," (48 FR 30263), such as that permitted on the nearby Delta Lake trail.

All plea offers to date have included a five-year ban from Grand Teton National Park, which would force the Defendant—a local bartender and professional mountain runner who regularly trains in the Tetons—to move from his home in Victor, ID.
The Constitution vests "[a]ll legislative Powers" in Congress, prohibiting delegation of "powers which are strictly and exclusively legislative," such as defining crimes.
